part: A Family Forged on the Plains
title: My Grandfather Was a Slave
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Diego on June 29, 2016, Roberta Alexander, born December 1946 in Berkeley, opens on the family that shaped her, her brother, and her sister, including a grandfather born in 1842 who had been a slave, rode the Pony Express on the promise to tell white people nothing of the Indians, and spoke Winnebago, Omaha, and Sioux while living between the Omaha Reservation and Sioux City, Iowa.

title: Riding the Rails to the Harvest
topic: The Grandfather and Her Father's Boyhood
summary: Alexander describes her grandfather as an itinerant Jew's harp musician, bootlegger, and cattle wrangler, rarely home yet father to ten children with her Scot Irish grandmother, and her father, born 1914, one of ten siblings, three sisters dead of the flu pandemic, tuberculosis, and a fire, a homeless boy of many jobs who at twelve hopped a freight from the reservation to seek harvest work in Minnesota.

title: Fifty Cents a Day
summary: Alexander tells how her father, twelve and hungry, walked five miles to a Minnesota farm, did the chores, and was paid fifty cents a day, until a Scandinavian bunkhouse worker who first called him a coon and a shine demanded the farmer pay him a dollar a day like the white men or face a strike.

title: A Little Red Card
summary: She describes the strike threat that won her father a dollar a day, the multiracial Sioux City packing-house strikes and Klan terror against strikers he had witnessed, and how at the harvest's end the worker enrolled him in the Industrial Workers of the World for his thirty dollars, leaving the young Black boy a member's red card.

title: Call Me by My Name
summary: Alexander reflects that white men standing up for her father's rights was formative for a boy raised among Native Americans and African Americans who knew few white people, recalls him asking the men to stop calling him shine and coon, and notes she learned the union details only through an oral history they recorded with him in 1975.

title: She Threw the Ink Well
summary: She recounts her father's grandmother as a fighter who, when a substitute teacher struck the children with a ruler, marched to school and threw an ink well at the woman, and who pulled her shoeless children out of school when they were denied entry, ending her father's education at the fifth grade.

title: From Vilna, Anti-Czar and Jewish
summary: Alexander traces her mother's family from Vilna in Lithuania, revolutionary anti-czar Jews who emigrated after the 1905 revolution, her bitter grandfather a St. Louis shoe-repair man who disowned her mother for marrying a Black man, and her grandmother, an illiterate garment worker who left him rather than disown her daughter, culturally Jewish, Yiddish-speaking, and progressive.

title: A Shoe Shine and a Manifesto
summary: She tells how her parents met at a Young Communist League party in Los Angeles, and how a young woman left her shoe-shine father a copy of the Communist Manifesto he could not yet read, his boss and a banker both dismissing it, until a welcoming, racially mixed party of people amazed by his background drew him into the movement and a real packing-house job at sixteen.

title: Hold the Line
topic: Packing-House Strike and the Civil Rights Congress
summary: Alexander describes her father's account of brutal Sioux City and Omaha packing-house labor, ten-to-thirteen-hour days amid blood and manure as the line sped from 250 to 350 animals, the wildcat strike he helped call, for which he was fired and blacklisted before coming to California, his organizing up and down the Central Valley for the CIO and the ILWU, and his directing the Civil Rights Congress in Oakland on police brutality and housing, holding that communists organized for basic people's rights rather than armed revolution, her parents leaving the party in the early 1960s.

title: My Father Went Underground
summary: Alexander, the youngest child, describes living with her uncle Mark in Perris, California, one of the few places Black people could buy land, after her father went underground during the McCarthy era, a frugal hardworking family of country kids who scavenged a grocery store's discards each week and whose apolitical uncle was fired from Camp Pendleton simply for being her father's brother.

title: Parked in the Sun
summary: She recalls the Pentecostal church the family attended several times a week, where people caught the holy ghost and spoke in tongues, the nightmares of a chasing devil it gave her, and the FBI cars that tailed her uncle until, to mess with them, he parked under a tree on a dead-end street and left the agents baking in hundred-degree heat.

title: A Shop Across From City Hall
summary: Alexander describes moving to Venice Beach near her grandmother and seeing her estranged grandfather just once, running away briefly at four or five missing her father, and her father, after a heart attack, learning shoe repair as an apprentice and opening shops, including one across from Los Angeles City Hall, where all five of them worked.

title: Shot Into in the Night
summary: She recounts her father being summoned before the House Un-American Activities Committee, his name in the Los Angeles Times, and the shoe shop being shot into months later, and the integration camp Anytown USA run by the National Conference of Christians and Jews, where her sister was elected mayor only to be stripped of the title once the director learned whose daughter she was.

title: Birdie, You Have to Understand
summary: Alexander remembers her first political conversation, when her father showed her the Jet magazine photographs of the murdered Emmett Till and told her things were not as she thought, leaving her horrified, and recalls a friend of her father's brother beaten and killed in police custody, for whose funeral the intimidated churches would not open their doors.

title: You're Negro, Don't Let Anybody Tell You Different
summary: She describes the stares the interracial family drew, her fair-skinned brother getting into fistfights insisting he was Negro, her father's instruction that they were Negro and should let no one say otherwise, and her arrival, the darkest of the siblings, at mixed Los Angeles High School where she took up folk dancing, guitar, and banjo.

part: Berkeley and the Free Speech Movement
title: I Arrived in Berkeley in 1964
summary: Alexander recounts graduating high school in 1964 after joining Los Angeles Woolworth's demonstrations, watching the Montgomery boycott and Southern sit-ins on the news from a distance, and choosing Berkeley because of its Sheraton Palace Hotel protests and movement ferment, getting in late on the strength of her senior grades.

title: Bettina Aptheker Took Me In
summary: She describes arriving with the dorms and co-ops full, her father introducing her to Bay Area movement friends like Jessica Mitford and Roscoe Proctor, and Bettina Aptheker, daughter of the communist scholar Herbert Aptheker, befriending her, planning her classes, and connecting her to the W.E.B. Du Bois Clubs of America and the Communist Party at Berkeley.

title: Arrested in Sproul Hall
summary: Alexander explains that the 1964 Free Speech Movement grew from Berkeley students mobilizing for the civil rights movement in Oakland and San Francisco, some of them returned Freedom Riders, and recounts her own arrest in Sproul Hall at seventeen, when she told police she was eighteen to avoid juvenile hall and spare her parents the trip, while Mario Savio and Bettina Aptheker spoke at near-daily rallies.

title: The Grises on Every Corner
summary: A Spanish major because of her Los Angeles upbringing, Alexander describes studying abroad in Madrid in 1966 and 1967 amid constant Berkeley activism, finding a repressive regime with gray-uniformed grises bearing machine guns, and setting up a table in the Facultad de Filosofia y Letras to gather signatures against the Vietnam War rather than risk touching Franco, with the press asking to be alerted to any action.

title: Yankis Fuera de Vietnam
topic: The Director and the Antiwar Speech
summary: She recalls her program director Carlos Blanco Aguinaga, a refugee of the Spanish Civil War, urging her to smuggle an antiwar sign to protest a visiting Ronald Reagan, the embassy warning it could not protect students at the April 28, 1967 demonstration, and the event in an occupied Madrid building hung with a banner reading Yankis fuera de Vietnam, where after chaos and a dead sound system she rose to a standing ovation before students rushed her out a back forest path to a taxi with instructions to flee to Andalusia.

title: We're the Plainclothes Police
summary: She recounts the Franco-sympathizer landlady warning that police had come looking, a cab driver recognizing her from a radio bulletin as the chica de colores, and being taken by plainclothes police down to the dungeon jail at the Puerta del Sol where prisoners rounded up before May Day sang We Shall Overcome, told at every level that the American embassy, not the Spanish state, wanted her removed.

title: A Limousine From CBS
topic: From Jail to the George V
summary: Alexander calls the expulsion more a kidnapping by the Spanish state under American orders, and describes the guarded train past Guardia Civil at every stop to Hendaye, where Carlos Blanco Aguinaga waited with money, the onward train to Paris sharing food with Moroccan workers, and a CBS man who whisked her, Carol Watanabe, and Carol Wynn to the Hotel George V for a friendly human-interest interview.

title: The Soviet Youth Was Fifty
summary: Back through New York to Berkeley, Alexander recounts disappointment that the Du Bois Clubs and Communist Party met her return with little fervor, a trip with Abraham Lincoln Brigade veterans to an East German celebration of the Nazis' fall where good food masked dead political conversation and a fifty-year-old stood in as the Soviet youth delegate, Cuban printers the one lively exception, all souring her on the Soviet bloc.

title: Black People Have to Join the Panthers
summary: Alexander describes passing through sectarian groups, including Bob Avakian's Revolutionary Union, which told her Black people had to join the Black Panther Party, and warming to the Panthers as they began community programs like free breakfast for school children, which she entered through the Oakland church where Charles Bursey was famously photographed, the leadership soon assigning the fast-typing recruit to the Black Panther newspaper.

title: Laying Out the Paper at the Printers
summary: She recounts round-the-clock layout sessions at a Tenderloin printer under editor Elbert Big Man Howard, where the party artist Emory Douglas brought his art, the full-time all-consuming life taking orders from a teenage officer of the day, and the harassment from men who framed sex as a revolutionary duty she refused, the relationship with Bobby Bowen from Richmond offering some protection.

title: Chivas Regal and Bitter Dog
summary: Alexander recalls Eldridge Cleaver already gone and his rationalized rape of white women, which struck her as no revolutionary act, names the male leadership of Bobby Seale, David Hilliard, Masai Hewitt, and Huey Newton above all, a class divide where leaders drank Chivas Regal and the rank and file drank Tony port and lemon juice called bitter dog, and recounts being told to drive David Hilliard home, his persistent advances over the ribs she was preparing for a picnic, and the crowd next morning assuming she had slept with him.

title: Women Are the Other Half
topic: Speaking at United Front Against Fascism
summary: Alexander recounts speaking on women at the United Front Against Fascism in the summer of 1969, building on Eldridge Cleaver's line that women are the other half, arguing the right place for Black women was inside the Panthers rather than the women's liberation movement, after which Angela Davis, not yet famous and sent by Carlos Blanco Aguinaga, introduced herself, and the party arranging a trip to Japan with Elbert Howard.

title: The Japanese Red Army Picked Us Up
summary: She describes going to Japan on tickets from the Zengakuren, agreeing to a tag-team approach favoring the Panthers' internationalist line over straight nationalism, a cloak-and-dagger airport pickup by people who turned out to be the Japanese Red Army whose railroad bombings the party had to disavow, the leftist film outfit Ogawa Productions taking them in hand, and earning their keep by charging for interviews on one-way tickets.

title: The Second and Third Sons
summary: Alexander explains the trip arose by accident when Japanese contacts offered a ticket in a chaotic, infiltrated 1969, and recounts spreading the Panther message and supporting Japanese movements with the film company's help, meeting little old women sitting in on US military airstrips, the second and third farm sons relegated to a separate caste doing construction work, and Korean immigrant workers, the party meanwhile having met leaders in China and Algeria.

title: They Threw the Night Soil
summary: She describes being followed everywhere except with one interpreter, the mass demonstrations of hundreds of thousands against a new airport built on prime farmland where she and Howard spoke, the Japanese protesters locking arms and running while elders flung night soil at netted police, and meeting peasant farmers, before Howard left for Europe and she returned alone after about a month.

title: They Would Vamp on the Office
topic: A Police Raid Plan and Three Reasons to Leave
summary: Alexander recounts a separate customs search on her return and refusing to surrender her telephone book, the metal-plated, sandbagged South Berkeley office and gun activities she avoided, firing a .357 Magnum once, and a Berkeley newspaper's discovery of the police plan to vamp on the office with fire engines and machine guns while she typed upstairs, and explains she walked away for three reasons: she did not want to die for the party, she believed it infiltrated by provocateurs, and she was pregnant, after which David Hilliard seized her library.

title: They Called It Lumumba-Zapata
summary: Back with her parents and typing for work, Alexander recounts Carlos Blanco Aguinaga calling her to San Diego to help launch what students including Angela Davis had won as Lumumba-Zapata College, a place to teach their own history, which the university renamed Third College, where at about twenty-three she was hired as resident dean for its first year.

title: Infiltrated From the Beginning
summary: She describes the college infiltrated from the start, administrators the university forced on the student-led committee, one sowing division by claiming it served whites and Chicanos rather than Black people, the intimidating alpha students who fixed her with unbroken stares in the fishbowl of the resident dean's house, her being attacked at a Black student union meeting for her wide-ranging background, suspecting COINTELPRO as with the Panthers, being threatened one night at gunpoint, and quitting after nine months.

title: A PhD Over Harvard
summary: She describes turning down a full Harvard scholarship as too cold and a little insulting and earning a PhD in comparative literature at UC San Diego on a Ford Foundation fellowship, with a dissertation on the fictional portrayal of popular movements across Haiti, Cuba, Mexico, and the United States, and reflects via the ribs and an uneaten tuna casserole on skin-color antagonism and the privilege read onto lighter-skinned Black people.

title: I Didn't Want the Academy
summary: Alexander recounts deciding against the academy's publish-or-perish competition and teaching adult-school ESL, much of it to Vietnamese refugees, for about twelve years, then being recruited to organize the ESL program at City College, where she spent twenty-five years, her chief contributions becoming English department chair and diversifying its faculty into a dedicated, functioning department.

title: They Wouldn't Send the People
summary: She describes running the floundering labor studies program with its associate's degree, introducing occupational safety and health classes taught with the UCLA labor program, and why it struggled in San Diego, union leaders reluctant to develop successors and the Central Labor Council praising the program but not sending students, contrasting it with LA Trade Tech's success, and notes she also taught happily in Chicano Studies.

title: We've Gone Back
summary: Alexander observes that Third College, now Thurgood Marshall, is no longer the revolutionary experiment it began as, that Black enrollment at UC campuses has fallen toward its 1964 lows despite affirmative action, and that packing houses, non-union and as dangerous as in her father's day, and police brutality and killings in Oakland show how far the country has slid back.

title: We Got It All Covered
summary: Alexander introduces Pillars of the Community, a faith-based Muslim organization in Encanto in Southeast San Diego she is active in, joking that her family, already Black and Jewish, gained Islam when her son converted twenty years ago, giving her Urdu-speaking grandsons, and describes its Encanto storefront, reminiscent of the old Panther storefronts, helping people leaving prison and organizing politically.

title: Penal Code 182.5
summary: She explains the organization's San Diego 33 campaign against California penal code 182.5, gang-documentation law by which wearing green, standing near a flagged house, or posting rap online can brand someone from age nine a gang member, and recounts the district attorney arresting thirty-three Black men, among them Aaron Harvey, held seven months on charges tied to violence he had nothing to do with though he was in Las Vegas, and how his family, joining Pillars of the Community, fought rather than accept the plea bargains most take in terror of fifty-year sentences, galvanizing a movement that also runs know-your-rights workshops.

title: Write Your Story
topic: A Storytelling Project and City Works Press
summary: She describes her role beginning with a meaningful rap album her Muslim son helped produce, then a storytelling program she helped start where people of color write one piece of their story and read it to a small gathering, done three times, surfacing harrowing and triumphant memories from a mother's murder to Aaron Harvey's account of being jailed himself, half the authors connected to City College, and the plan to publish nineteen of them through the independent City Works Press with a blurb from Elbert Howard and ties to writers Jimmy Santiago Baca and Luis Rodriguez.

part: Reflections on a Life in Movements
title: I'm Not a Star
summary: She describes speaking to classes and panels at San Diego State and UC San Diego, often amid organizing, including after the Compton Cookout, nooses, and anti-Black graffiti incidents, presenting Elbert Howard when he visited, and insisting she is not a star but someone who happened to be in many places doing things without knowing the risks, a serious year in the Panthers among them.

title: My Way to Deal Is to Forget
summary: Alexander reflects that the Panthers did not take care of themselves, leaving no time for relationships among women and no space to discuss the sexual harassment, and that she copes with high stress, her worst being Third College, by forgetting, having blocked out the gunpoint threat her director's family still remembers, an emotional toll she suppressed to keep going.
keywords: did not take care of ourselves

title: I Walk Through the World as African American
summary: She reflects on being born out of wedlock because interracial marriage was illegal, the difficulty of mixed race in a time of identity politics, her closeness to her Jewish grandmother who opposed Israeli policy toward Palestine, her refusal of strictly race-based gatherings she is always invited to, and her admiration that the Panthers reached out to other groups rather than fighting over crumbs.

title: The Solid Rock Behind It All
summary: Alexander closes on her siblings, a sister who devoted her life to health care in the African American community and a brother who, after suffering most in the McCarthy era, became chief medical officer of a Kaiser section in Los Angeles, and on her one regret, that she interviewed her aunt, brother, sister, and cousins but never her mother, the quiet solid rock who held the family together.
